description: Golden set v2: 500-paper UHTC corpus, baseline-vs-agentic benchmark. Generated by Claude agents from source PDFs (Haiku for tier A/C, Sonnet-class for tier B), assembled by eval/golden_v2_work/assemble.py. Evidence spans are verbatim PDF text; run eval/retrieval_eval.py --verify against the index and repair non-matching spans before freezing. FROZEN after human review — do not tune against the test split.

questions.question: In the Hoque et al. 2023 study on high-entropy metal diboride (Hf0.2Zr0.2Ti0.2Ta0.2Nb0.2)B2, what was the room-temperature thermal conductivity value measured for the HEB specimen?
questions.reference_answer: The room-temperature thermal conductivity of the HEB sample is 27.8 ± 2.9 W m−1 K−1, as reported in Table 1 of Hoque et al. (2023). This value is significantly lower than monolithic diborides due to extensive electron and phonon scattering from lattice distortions and compositional disorder.
evidence_any: The room-temperature thermal conductivity of the HEB sample is 27.8 ± 2.9

questions.question: According to Hoque et al. (2023), what maximum temperature was reached when measuring the thermal conductivity of the high-entropy diboride (Hf0.2Zr0.2Ti0.2Ta0.2Nb0.2)B2 using laser flash analysis (LFA)?
questions.reference_answer: The laser flash analysis (LFA) measurements reached a maximum temperature of 1267 °C to measure thermal diffusivity of the HEB, from which thermal conductivity was extracted and compared with SSTR and TDTR measurements at lower temperatures.
evidence_any: LFA is used to measure thermal diffusivity up to 1267 ◦C

questions.question: In the Hoque et al. (2023) paper on (Hf0.2Zr0.2Ti0.2Ta0.2Nb0.2)B2, what was the sintering temperature and duration used during the reactive spark plasma sintering process?
questions.reference_answer: The final sintering temperature was 2200 °C held for 30 minutes, reached by ramping at 30 °C/min after initial outgassing holds at 1400 and 1600 °C for 80 minutes each. A pressure of 80 MPa was applied at the maximum temperature.
evidence_any: final sintering temperature of 2200 ◦C and held for 30 min

questions.question: What was the measured electrical conductivity of the high-entropy diboride (Hf0.2Zr0.2Ti0.2Ta0.2Nb0.2)B2 specimen reported by Hoque et al. (2023), and what does this value correspond to for electron contribution to thermal conductivity?
questions.reference_answer: The electrical conductivity of the HEB specimen was (2.42 ± 0.12) × 10^6 S/m, which corresponds to an electron contribution of 18.1 ± 0.9 W m−1 K−1, representing nearly 65% of the total thermal conductivity of HEB.
evidence_any: The measured electrical conductivity of the HEB specimen is (2.42 ± 0.12) × 106 S/m

questions.question: In the Hoque et al. (2023) ablation study, what were the measured laser ablation thresholds reported for the high-entropy diboride (Hf0.2Zr0.2Ti0.2Ta0.2Nb0.2)B2 and ZrB2, and what does this indicate about their bond strengths?
questions.reference_answer: The ablation thresholds were 0.018 ± 0.005 J/cm² for HEB and 0.023 ± 0.009 J/cm² for ZrB2, which are nearly the same within uncertainty, indicating that HEB and ZrB2 possess very similar bond strengths and hence nearly identical thermal ablation resistances.
evidence_any: ablation thresholds are measured to be 0.018 ± 0.005 and 0.023 ± 0.009

questions.question: According to the Hoque et al. (2023) paper, what was the average grain size of the high-entropy diboride specimen, and what crystal structure does this material possess?
questions.reference_answer: The average grain size of the HEB specimen was 15 ± 9.5 μm as measured by electron backscatter diffraction (EBSD). The HEB possessed a single-phase, hexagonal crystal structure (AlB2) with high symmetry.
evidence_any: The average grain size of the HEB specimen was 15±9.5 μm

questions.question: In the Kelly et al. (2022) binder jet additive manufacturing study on ZrB2-MoSi2 ceramic heat exchangers, what target relative density was specified as the goal for the fabricated parts, and what is the significance of this density level?
questions.reference_answer: Kelly et al. aimed to achieve parts with relative density ≥ 92% of theoretical, which indicates the transition from intermediate to final stage sintering and is a requirement to inhibit gas permeability and enable sintering of complex shapes to full density with sinter-HIP technology.
evidence_any: We aimed to achieve parts with a relative density ≥ 92 % of theoretical

questions.question: What particle size range was observed for the ZrB2 and MoSi2 powders selected for binder jet printing in the Kelly et al. (2022) study, and what manufacturing objectives did the choice of these fine particles support?
questions.reference_answer: Most particles were in the 1-5 μm range with no particles greater than 7 μm observed. These finer particle sizes were selected to retain good sintering characteristics and better properties while pushing the limits of what is typical for binder jet additive manufacturing.
evidence_any: Most of the particles were in the 1–5 µm range and no particles greater than 7 µm

questions.question: According to Kelly et al. (2022), what sintering temperature and duration were ultimately used to achieve the target density for ZrB2-MoSi2 TPMS structures, and what volumetric shrinkage resulted from this process?
questions.reference_answer: Parts were sintered at 2000 °C for 18 hours in argon, achieving densities ranging from 93-98% of theoretical. The volumetric shrinkage was approximately 60% and was isotropic with insignificant difference between length, width, and height of the cubic parts.
evidence_any: sintering three samples at 2000ºC for 18 hrs resulted in densities ranging from 5.65

questions.question: In the Kelly et al. (2022) study of binder jet-printed ZrB2-MoSi2 TPMS heat exchangers, what estimated power density was calculated for a heat exchanger based on Schwarz-D TPMS dimensions, and what maximum heat exchanger capacity could theoretically be produced with current commercial printers?
questions.reference_answer: The calculated power density for the UHTC-TPMS heat exchanger based on Schwarz-D dimensions and molten chloride salt TES inputs was up to 50 MW/m³. With the largest commercial binder jet printer's build volume, this would enable a one-piece heat exchanger with capacity rating up to 3 MW.
evidence_any: power density of up to 50 MW/m3 for a UHTC-TPMS heat exchanger

questions.question: What challenge related to warping was identified during the sintering process in Kelly et al. (2022), and what mitigation strategy proved effective for preventing distortion of the ZrB2-MoSi2 parts?
questions.reference_answer: Parts experienced significant warping during sintering accompanied by approximately 6% mass loss, suggesting a vaporization-driven distortion process. Spatial confinement in a carbon powder bed packing inside inverted crucibles effectively mitigated warping and eliminated the observed dimensional distortion.
evidence_any: spatial confinement in a carbon packing was able to effectively mitigate warping

questions.question: Which element segregates to grain boundaries in the medium entropy carbide TiZrHfC3 according to Dai et al. 2022?
questions.reference_answer: According to Dai et al. 2022, all investigated grain boundaries in TiZrHfC3 medium entropy carbide are enriched with titanium (Ti), which is mainly complemented by depletion of hafnium (Hf).
evidence_any: For the medium entropy TiZrHfC3, all the investigated GBs are enriched with Ti

questions.question: What cutoff distance was used in the deep potential model for multi-element transition metal carbides in Dai et al. 2022?
questions.reference_answer: In Dai et al. 2022, the deep potential model used a cutoff distance of 6.5 Ångström with a smooth function imposed from 6.0 Ångström.
evidence_any: the cutoff was set to be 6.5 ˚A with a smooth function imposed from 6.0 ˚A

questions.question: How frequently were metal atom pairs swapped during Monte Carlo simulations in Dai et al. 2022 segregation study?
questions.reference_answer: In Dai et al. 2022, during Monte Carlo simulations for grain boundary segregation, a pair of metal atoms were swapped once per 2 steps.
evidence_any: During MC, a pair of metal atoms were swapped once per 2 steps

questions.question: What were the energy and force prediction errors of the deep potential model trained by Dai et al. 2022 compared to DFT calculations?
questions.reference_answer: The deep potential model in Dai et al. 2022 achieved prediction errors of 15 meV/atom in energy and 400 meV/Ångström in force when compared to DFT calculations.
evidence_any: prediction errors in energy and force are 15 meV/atom and 400 meV

questions.question: What plane-wave basis set cutoff energy was used in the DFT calculations for Dai et al. 2022?
questions.reference_answer: The DFT calculations performed by Dai et al. 2022 using the CASTEP computational package employed a plane-wave basis set cutoff energy of 500 eV.
evidence_any: The cutoff energy of the plane-wave basis set was set to be 500 eV

questions.question: What was the sampling frequency used for analyzing grain boundary segregation configurations in Dai et al. 2022?
questions.reference_answer: Dai et al. 2022 analyzed segregation by averaging Monte Carlo/molecular dynamics simulated configurations with a sampling frequency of every 100 steps from the simulation steps 250,000 to 500,000.
evidence_any: averaging the MC/MD simulated configurations from step 250,000 to 500,000 with sampling frequency being every 100 steps

questions.question: How many total Monte Carlo simulation steps were performed in Dai et al. 2022 segregation simulations?
questions.reference_answer: In Dai et al. 2022, the Monte Carlo simulations for grain boundary segregation equilibration used a total of 500,000 steps.
evidence_any: The total steps of MC simulation were 500,000.

questions.question: Which elements show enrichment and depletion at grain boundaries in the high entropy carbide TiZrHfNbTaC5 according to Dai et al. 2022?
questions.reference_answer: In Dai et al. 2022, the high entropy TiZrHfNbTaC5 carbide shows grain boundary segregation patterns where grain boundaries are mainly segregated with either titanium (Ti) or zirconium (Zr), which is mainly compensated by depletion of hafnium (Hf) and/or tantalum (Ta).
evidence_any: TiZrHfNbTaC5, the results are more complicated, where the 3(112), 5(210)

questions.question: In Khoeini et al. 2015 study on ZrB2-SiC composites with pressureless sintering, what maximum relative density was achieved in the ZS10C2 composite sample?
questions.reference_answer: The maximum relative density of 99.65% was achieved in the ZS10C2 composite sample, which was sintered at 2000°C for 2 hours after holding at 1650°C for 2 hours. This represented among the highest densities obtained through pressureless sintering in that study.
evidence_any: maximum relative density of 99.65% has been achieved in ZS10C2 composite

questions.question: According to Khoeini et al. 2015, what is the melting temperature of zirconium diboride (ZrB2)?
questions.reference_answer: Zirconium diboride (ZrB2) has a melting temperature of 3250°C. This ultra-high melting temperature is one of the key characteristics that makes ZrB2 of interest for high-temperature structural applications.
evidence_any: Zirconium diboride (ZrB2) an ultra-high temperature ceramic with melting temperature of 3250 1C

questions.question: In the Khoeini et al. 2015 pressureless sintering study, at what temperature was the maximum relative density achieved for ZrB2-SiC composites?
questions.reference_answer: The optimum temperature to achieve maximum relative density by pressureless sintering of ZrB2-SiC composites was 2000°C. Sintering at temperatures of 1800°C or 2100°C resulted in lower relative densities compared to 2000°C.
evidence_any: optimum temperature to achieve maximum relative density by pressureless sintering is 2000 1C

questions.question: What were the amounts of open-type and closed-type porosities measured in the Nayebi et al. 2016 ZrB2-40 vol% B4C composite?
questions.reference_answer: In the Nayebi et al. 2016 study, the amounts of open-type and closed-type porosities in the microstructure of the ZrB2-40 vol% B4C composite were calculated to be 24% and 7%, respectively. These two types of porosities were initiated from different factors during the hot pressing process.
evidence_any: The amounts of open-type and closed-type porosities in the microstructure of ZrB2–40 vol% B4C ceramic composite were calculated to be ≈24% and 7%, respectively.

questions.question: According to Nayebi et al. 2016, what were the average particle sizes of the ZrB2 and B4C powders used in the hot pressing process?
questions.reference_answer: In the Nayebi et al. 2016 study, commercially available ZrB2 powder had an average particle size of 2 micrometers from Leung Hi-tech Co., China, while B4C powder had an average particle size of 20 micrometers from Mudanjiang Jingangzuan Boron Carbide Co., China.
evidence_any: ZrB2 powder with an average particle size of 2 μm (Leung Hi-tech Co., China) and B4C powder with an average particle size of 20 μm

questions.question: In the Nayebi et al. 2016 hot pressing study, what temperature and pressure conditions were used to sinter the ZrB2-B4C composite?
questions.reference_answer: The Nayebi et al. 2016 study used hot pressing at 1800°C for 30 minutes under an external pressure of 12 MPa to sinter the ZrB2-40 vol% B4C composite. The sample was initially heated at 15°C/min to 1000°C, held for 30 minutes, then heated at 12°C/min to reach 1800°C.
evidence_any: An external pressure of 12 MPa was applied to the sample, for 30 min, after the temperature reached 1800 °C.

questions.question: What microhardness value was achieved for the ZS10C composite in Khoeini et al. 2015 when sintered at 2000°C for 2 hours?
questions.reference_answer: The ZS10C composite sintered at 2000°C for 2 hours achieved a microhardness value of 4502 Hv (Vickers hardness). This was significantly higher than the values recorded for the same composite sintered at 1800°C (3520 Hv) or 2100°C (3860 Hv).
evidence_any: the average values of microhardness obtained for the ZS10C composite sintered for 2h at 1800 1C and 2100 1C are 3520Hv and 3860Hv, respectively

questions.question: According to Nayebi et al. 2016, what liquid-phase compound forms at the sintering temperature of 1800°C in the ZrB2-B4C system?
questions.reference_answer: Diboron trioxide (B2O3) is the only liquid-state phase that can be formed at the sintering temperature of 1800°C in the ZrB2-B4C system, and it commonly solidifies as an amorphous phase. This amorphous B2O3 phase is detected on the surfaces of B4C particles and at ZrB2/B4C interfaces.
evidence_any: diboron trioxide is the only liquid-state phase which can be formed at sintering temperature of 1800 °C in ZrB2–B4C system

questions.question: In the Khoeini et al. 2015 study, how much did the microhardness of the composites increase as the SiC content was increased from 1 to 10 wt%?
questions.reference_answer: The microhardness of the composites sintered for 2 hours increased by 55.5% as their SiC content was increased from 1 to 10 wt%. This increase was attributed to the combined effects of SiC's higher hardness compared to the ZrB2 matrix, decreased ZrB2 grain size, and increased densification with higher SiC content.
evidence_any: the average microhardness of the composites sintered for 2 h increases by 55.5%, as their SiC content is increased from1 to 10 wt%

questions.question: What chemical reaction did Nayebi et al. 2016 propose occurs at the ZrB2/B4C interfaces to form submicron ZrB2 particles?
questions.reference_answer: Nayebi et al. 2016 proposed the reaction: 7ZrO2 + 5B4C → 7ZrB2 + 3B2O3(l) + 5CO(g). This reaction produces submicron ZrB2 particles that can float in the liquid boria (B2O3), potentially migrating from the ZrB2/B4C grain interfaces to other regions in the composite.
evidence_any: 7ZrO2 þ 5B4C - 7ZrB2 þ 3B2O3 (l) þ 5CO (g)

questions.question: According to Nayebi et al. 2016, what mechanisms control the sintering process in the hot-pressed ZrB2-B4C composite?
questions.reference_answer: Nayebi et al. 2016 identified that the sintering process in hot-pressed ZrB2-B4C composite is controlled by a combination of interfacial phenomena including: formation of liquid phase at interfaces, plastic deformation of ZrB2 particles, and diffusion of carbon and/or boron atoms from B4C toward ZrB2 particles. These mechanisms work together to promote densification despite the presence of significant porosity.
evidence_any: the sintering process is controlled by a combination of interfacial phenomena including the formation of liquid phase

questions.question: In Zapata-Solvas 2018, what were the calculated activation energies for the 20 vol% SiC-HfB2 (HS20) composite at different temperature ranges during compressive creep testing?
questions.reference_answer: The activation energies for Zapata-Solvas 2018's HS20 composite were 242 kJ/mol in the temperature range 1500-1800°C and 334 kJ/mol in the range 1900-2000°C, indicating a change in the active deformation mechanism with increasing temperature.
evidence_any: calculated activation energies are 242 and 334 kJ/mol in the tem

questions.question: What powder particle sizes and densities were used in Zapata-Solvas 2018 for the HfB2, SiC, and La2O3 starting materials in the HS20 and HS20La composites?
questions.reference_answer: In Zapata-Solvas 2018, HfB2 powder had d50 ~5.0 μm and ρ = 10.5 g/cm³, SiC powder (α-SiC) had d50 ~0.7 μm and ρ = 3.217 g/cm³, and La2O3 had d50 ~10 μm and ρ = 6.51 g/cm³.
evidence_any: HfB2 powder (> 99%, d50 ∼ 5.0 μm, ρ = 10.5 g/cm3

questions.question: What is the thermal conductivity range reported for ZrB2 and HfB2 diborides in Zapata-Solvas 2018 at high temperatures?
questions.reference_answer: According to Zapata-Solvas 2018, ZrB2 and HfB2 exhibit higher thermal conductivity of approximately 100 W/(m K) at room temperature and greater than 50 W/(m K) at 1900°C, which is higher than their respective carbides (less than 40 W/(m K) from room temperature to 800°C).
evidence_any: ZrB2 and HfB2 exhibit higher thermal conductivity (∼100 W/(m K))

questions.question: In Zapata-Solvas 2018, what was the elastic modulus value used for HS20 when calculating thermal strain rates for hypersonic heating conditions?
questions.reference_answer: For Zapata-Solvas 2018's HS20 composite, an elastic modulus of approximately 500 GPa was used when estimating thermal strain rates related to heating rates of 200-1000°C/min for hypersonic conditions.
evidence_any: at an elastic modulus of ∼500 GPa [4]

questions.question: According to Zapata-Solvas 2018, what phase was detected on the external surface of HS20 samples at temperatures higher than 1800°C during compressive deformation?
questions.reference_answer: In Zapata-Solvas 2018, formation of HfB (hafnium monoboride) was detected on the external surface of HS20 samples at temperatures higher than 1800°C as revealed by X-ray diffraction analysis, although it was not observed in the internal bulk.
evidence_any: at temperatures higher than 1800 °C, the formation of HfB is detected

questions.question: In Zapata-Solvas 2018, what maximum value of n in SiBn particles was observed in HS20La (HfB2+20vol%SiC+2wt%La2O3) after deformation at 2000°C?
questions.reference_answer: In Zapata-Solvas 2018, SiBn particles with n=12 were detected in HS20La after deformation at 2000°C, with some particles containing minor amounts of La, indicating that La played a role in forming new SiBn phases during high-temperature deformation.
evidence_any: the maximum n in HS20 is 2 whereas it is 12 for HS20La

questions.question: In Dai et al. 2022, what constant heat flux value was applied to the outer surface in the verification example to test the numerical accuracy of the DQM and Newmark method solution?
questions.reference_answer: In Dai et al. 2022's verification example, a constant heat flux of 300 kW/m² was applied to the outer surface of the multilayer hollow cylinder structure to validate the accuracy of the differential quadrature method (DQM) and Newmark method numerical solution approach.
evidence_any: The heat flux exerted on the outer surface is qw = 300 kW/m²

questions.question: According to Dai et al. 2022, what multilayer structure was used for the thermal protection system (TPS) of the cylindrical cabin, including the materials of each layer?
questions.reference_answer: In Dai et al. 2022, the multilayer thermal protection system (TPS) consisted of three layers: an FG-UHTC (functionally graded ultrahigh-temperature ceramic) outer layer for heat resistance, a SiC aerogel middle layer for thermal insulation, and a TC4 inner layer for load bearing.
evidence_any: FG-UHTC) outer layer for heat resistance, a SiC aerogel middle layer

questions.question: In Dai et al. 2022, what volume fraction equations were used to describe the gradient distributions of ZrC nanoparticles and micro pores in the FG-UHTC composite?
questions.reference_answer: In Dai et al. 2022, power law expressions were used to characterize the gradient distributions of ZrC and micro pores: V_ZrC(r) = V₀_ZrC[1-(R₀+h-r)/h_c]^n_ZrC and V_p(r) = V₀_p[(R₀+h-r)/h_c]^n_p, allowing independent control of component gradation in the radial direction.
evidence_any: power law can be used to characterize this kind of gradient distributions

questions.question: In the Hong et al. 2009 study on ultra-high-porosity zirconia ceramics, what were the porosity and compressive strength values for samples produced from different zirconia contents?
questions.reference_answer: Hong et al. (2009) report that porosity varied between 82% and 67% as the solid loading was increased, while compressive strength was significantly improved from 41 to 63 MPa. Specifically, samples with 10 vol.% solid content showed 82% porosity and 41.4 ± 1.8 MPa strength, while 20 vol.% samples achieved 67% porosity and 62.8 ± 3.1 MPa strength.
evidence_any: The porosity varied between 82% and 67%, while the compressive strength was significantly improved from 41 to 63 MPa

questions.question: What was the pore size range observed in the porous zirconia ceramics fabricated by Hong et al. 2009 using freeze-casting?
questions.reference_answer: Hong et al. (2009) report that the freeze-cast zirconia ceramics exhibited moderate pores with sizes ranging from 20 to 50 μm. This pore size was maintained across samples with different solid loadings.
evidence_any: All of the fabricated samples showed highly porous microstructures with moderate pores ranging from 20 to 50 μm in size

questions.question: What freezing vehicle and composition were used in the freeze-casting process described by Hong et al. 2009 for producing porous zirconia?
questions.reference_answer: Hong et al. (2009) used camphene (C10H16) as the freezing vehicle. The zirconia/camphene slurries were prepared with zirconia contents of 10, 15, and 20 vol.%, with camphene serving as the medium that would be sublimed away during freeze-drying.
evidence_any: camphene (C10H16, Guangzhou Huangpu Chemical Factory, Guangzhou, China) was used as the freezing vehicle

questions.question: What was the yttria dopant concentration in the zirconia ceramic powder used by Hong et al. 2009?
questions.reference_answer: Hong et al. (2009) used yttria-stabilized zirconia doped with 5 mol.% Y2O3. The powder had a particle size of 0.4 μm and was sourced from Fanmeiya Powders Co. Ltd., Jiangxi, China.
evidence_any: Commercially available yttria-stabilized zirconia doped with 5 mol.% Y2O3 (0.4 μm, Fanmeiya

questions.question: In the Makurunje et al. 2017 study of Cf/C-SiC-TiC-TaC composites, what was the nominal heat flux and duration of the oxyacetylene flame test?
questions.reference_answer: Makurunje et al. (2017) exposed the Cf/C-SiC-TiC-TaC composite to an oxyacetylene flame shot of 7.5 s with a nominal heat flux of 4 MW/m2. This test was performed according to the ASTM E285-80 (2002) standard test method.
evidence_any: An oxyacetylene flame shot of 7.5 s (4 MW/m2 nominal heat flux)

questions.question: What were the temperature and maximum exposure time used in the oxidation testing of Cf/C and Cf/C-STT composites by Makurunje et al. 2017?
questions.reference_answer: Makurunje et al. (2017) studied oxidation resistance in air at 1873 K for up to 480 s nominal exposure time using a radiant furnace. Samples were exposed for various durations: 60 s, 120 s, 240 s, and 480 s.
evidence_any: oxidising conditions imposed by a radiant furnace in air at 1873 K up to 480 s

questions.question: What were the bulk densities of the Cf/C and Cf/C-STT composites reported by Makurunje et al. 2017?
questions.reference_answer: Makurunje et al. (2017) report that the uninfiltrated Cf/C composite had a bulk density of 1.54 g/cm3, while the Cf/C-STT composite with the infiltrated alloy achieved a bulk density of 2.83 g/cm3.
evidence_any: The Cf/C and Cf/C-STT composites had densities of 1.54 g/cm3 and 2.83 g/cm3, respectively

questions.question: To what maximum temperature was the Cf/C composite heated during spark plasma sintering in the Makurunje et al. 2017 study?
questions.reference_answer: Makurunje et al. (2017) heated the Cf/C composite with the alloy powder in a spark plasma sintering (SPS) furnace to a maximum temperature of 1973 K in a vacuum range of 10–100 Pa. The alloy powder was 66.7Si-17.9Ti-15.4Ta (−40 μm particle size).
evidence_any: the two were heated in a spark plasma sintering (SPS) furnace in a vacuum range of 10–100 Pa up to 1973 K

questions.question: What were the measured mass ablation rates for Cf/C and Cf/C-STT composites exposed to oxyacetylene flame for 7.5 s according to Makurunje et al. 2017?
questions.reference_answer: Makurunje et al. (2017) measured mass ablation rates (Rm) of 40.2 ± 0.1 mg/s for the unprotected Cf/C composite and 3.9 ± 0.1 mg/s for the Cf/C-STT composite during 7.5 s oxyacetylene flame exposure at 4 MW/m2 nominal heat flux. The Cf/C-STT composite showed approximately a ten-fold reduction in ablation rate.
evidence_any: Mass (Rm) and linear (Rl,max) ablation rates of Cf/C and Cf/C-STT composites exposed to the oxyacetylene flame for 7.5 s

questions.question: By what factor did the mass loss of the Cf/C-STT composite differ from the unprotected Cf/C composite during oxidation testing at 1873 K in the Makurunje et al. 2017 study?
questions.reference_answer: Makurunje et al. (2017) found that the Cf/C-STT composite exhibited net mass loss approximately one order of magnitude lower than the unprotected Cf/C composite when tested in air at 1873 K. The SiC and (Ta,Ti)C ceramic matrix additions provided substantial protection against oxidative attack.
evidence_any: a net mass loss (Δm) about one order of magnitude lower than that of Cf/C composites

questions.question: In Ren et al. 2017's study on HfC nanowire-reinforced coatings, what was the estimated length of the synthesized HfC nanowires formed on the SiC coating surface?
questions.reference_answer: According to Ren et al. (2017), the HfC nanowires synthesized on the SiC coating via chemical vapor deposition formed a porous layer with lengths of approximately several hundred micrometers. The high-magnification image revealed that the nanowire diameter was 200–500 nm.
evidence_any: Plentiful long and straight nanowires form a porous layer, whose length is about several hundred micrometers.

questions.question: In the Ren et al. 2017 CVD synthesis of HfC nanowires, what was the temperature range maintained in the high-temperature deposition chamber?
questions.reference_answer: Ren et al. (2017) synthesized HfC nanowires via chemical vapor deposition by heating the high-temperature chamber to 1373–1473 K and maintaining that temperature for 3 hours to achieve nanowire formation on the SiC-coated C/C composite substrates.
evidence_any: The high-temperature deposition chamber was heated to 1373–1473 K and maintained for 3 h.

questions.question: In Ren et al. 2017's scratch test, at what load did the coating with HfC nanowires show the first detectable acoustic emission signal indicating debonding?
questions.reference_answer: According to Ren et al. (2017), the first obvious acoustic emission signal indicating debonding of the outer ZrB2-SiC coating with HfC nanowires occurred at 7.5 N during the scratch test, compared to 5.9 N for the coating without nanowires, demonstrating improved interface bonding strength.
evidence_any: the first obvious AE signal is detected at 7.5 N for the coating with nanowires

questions.question: According to Vinci et al. 2025, what was the peak temperature and duration for which it was maintained during the vacuum annealing of the niobium-coated composite?
questions.reference_answer: Vinci et al. (2025) conducted vacuum annealing of the Nb-coated composites with the temperature held at 1800°C for 60 minutes, then cooled rapidly to 1750°C and held for an additional 180 minutes to promote interdiffusion and coating adhesion.
evidence_any: the temperature was held for 60 min at 1800 °C, cooled rapidly to 1750

questions.question: What melting point is reported for Ta0.8Hf0.2C ceramic in Ghaffari et al. (2013) pressureless sintering study with MoSi2?
questions.reference_answer: Ghaffari et al. (2013) reports that Ta0.8Hf0.2C ceramic has a melting point of 4000°C, which is the highest among known materials.
evidence_any: Ta0.8Hf0.2C ceramic has the highest melting point among the known materials (4000 1C)

questions.question: What MoSi2 content and resulting relative density were optimal for Ta0.8Hf0.2C sintering at 2000°C in Ghaffari et al. (2013)?
questions.reference_answer: In Ghaffari et al. (2013), 24 vol.% MoSi2 was the optimum amount, yielding the highest relative density of 95% for Ta0.8Hf0.2C sintered at 2000°C for 1 hour.
evidence_any: It seems that MoSi2 addition above 24 vol.% does not increase the final density, noticeably. Therefore, it can be concluded that 24 vol.% is an optimum amount for MoSi2 as sintering aid.

questions.question: What protective oxide layers formed on Ta0.8Hf0.2C/24 vol.% MoSi2 during flame oxidation testing in Ghaffari et al. (2013)?
questions.reference_answer: Ghaffari et al. (2013) observed two protective layers during flame oxidation: an outer porous Ta and Hf oxide layer (50 μm thickness) and an inner Ta-Hf-Mo oxycarbide diffusion barrier layer (10-30 μm thickness).
evidence_any: formation of two different protective layers on the top surface of sample after the flame test, an outer porous layer with 50 μm thickness and an inner layer with 10–30 μm thickness

questions.question: How did HfC content affect the relative density of TaC-based composites in Ghaffari et al. (2013)?
questions.reference_answer: In Ghaffari et al. (2013), increasing HfC content from 0% to 20 vol.% in TaC with fixed 12 vol.% MoSi2 caused the final relative density to decrease to 80%.
evidence_any: the final relative density decreases down to 80% when HfC content increases up to 20 vol.%

questions.question: What advantages did the colloidal process provide for carbon fiber-toughened ZrB2–ZrSi2 in Sha et al. (2016)?
questions.reference_answer: In Sha et al. (2016), the colloidal process stabilized fibers and ceramic powder in solvent, enabling homogeneous fiber dispersion while avoiding ball-milling damage.
evidence_any: By colloidal process, the short fibers and ceramic powder can be stabilized in a solvent, which is possible to disperse the short fibers into matrix homogeneously

questions.question: What relative densities were achieved for CZS1 and CZS2 composites in Sha et al. (2016)?
questions.reference_answer: Sha et al. (2016) achieved relative densities of 94.16% for CZS1 (conventional processing) and 98.35% for CZS2 (colloidal process) in carbon fiber-toughened ZrB2–ZrSi2 composites.
evidence_any: The relative densities are 94.16% for CZS1 and 98.35% for CZS2, which correspond to an open porosity of 2.27% and 0.57%, respectively

questions.question: What flexural strengths were measured for CZS1 and CZS2 composites in Sha et al. (2016)?
questions.reference_answer: Sha et al. (2016) measured flexural strengths of 376 ± 21 MPa for CZS1 and 458 ± 11 MPa for CZS2 carbon fiber-toughened ZrB2–ZrSi2 composites.
evidence_any: The flexural strengths are 376 ± 21 MPa for CZS1 and 458 ± 11 MPa for CZS2, respectively

questions.question: What electrical resistivity per unit dimension does the Li/Ce-doped La2Ti2O7:0.2MnO2 ceramic retain when exposed to 1000 degrees Celsius?
questions.reference_answer: At 1000 degrees Celsius, LLCTO:0.2Mn maintains a resistivity of approximately 2.4 × 10^7 ohm-centimeters. This high electrical resistance at extreme temperature is consistent with the material's ability to preserve strong piezoelectric performance in the 1000-1200 degree Celsius range.
evidence_any: resistivity of \~2.4 × 10^7 Ω.cm even at 1000 ◦C

questions.question: How does the introduction of manganese in the La1.97Li0.015Ce0.015Ti2O7:0.2MnO2 system influence the switching behavior of electric domains within the crystal lattice?
questions.reference_answer: Manganese acts as a soft dopant that enhances ferroelectric behavior by facilitating easier orientation of charge domain mobility. This soft-agent effect enables more facile domain switching, which explains the significant improvement in the piezoelectric d33 coefficient from 6.6 pC/N (pristine LLCTO) to 9 pC/N (LLCTO:0.2Mn).
evidence_any: the Mn additive, which possesses the ability to act as a soft agent

questions.question: In which aerospace applications have TiB2, ZrB2, and HfB2 diborides been proposed for use as thermal insulating systems?
questions.reference_answer: Group 4 diborides are proposed for thermal protection systems in hypersonic aircraft, atmospheric re-entry vehicles for space missions, and rocket propulsion systems. These applications exploit the materials' exceptional melting temperatures and resistance to thermal shock in environments with extreme aerothermal heating.
evidence_any: as thermal protection systems in hypersonic flight, atmospheric re-entry vehicles, and rocket propulsion

questions.question: What specific surface phenomena between Group 4 metal diborides and transition metal components have been experimentally characterized to understand their interaction mechanisms?
questions.reference_answer: Wettability (the degree to which liquid spreads on a solid surface) and interfacial tension at the contact interface between diborides and metals have been studied experimentally. These properties, along with joining behavior, provide crucial information about how well the ceramic and metallic components bond and interact at high temperatures.
evidence_any: Wettability and interfacial tension of Group 4 diborides in contact with metals

questions.question: In the 2013 research by Zapata-Solvas et al. measuring how heat transport through ultra-high-temperature diboride ceramics changes across elevated temperatures, why did adding lanthanum oxide to silicon-carbide-reinforced zirconium diboride materials produce markedly different heat flow behavior compared to silicon-carbide-reinforced zirconium diboride alone in the 1500-1900°C operating window?
questions.reference_answer: In Zapata-Solvas et al. 2013, La2O3-doped ZrB2-SiC composites maintained nearly constant thermal conductivity between 1500°C and 1900°C, whereas SiC-doped ZrB2 without La2O3 showed a 7% decrease in thermal conductivity over the same temperature interval. This stability in the rare-earth-doped composites resulted from La2O3 particles preventing contact loss between SiC grains and matrix grains, thereby maintaining consistent Kapitza resistance values.
evidence_any: Thermal conductivity was calculated from RT to 1900 ◦C for each UHTC

questions.question: In SHAO et al. 2023, what were the primary crystalline phases identified in the oxidized surface layers formed on the zirconium carbide-silicon carbide-molybdenum disilicide coatings after exposure to oxyacetylene flames across all three heating conditions?
questions.reference_answer: In SHAO et al. 2023, the oxidized surfaces of the ZSM coatings after ablation were primarily composed of monoclinic zirconia (m-ZrO2) and zirconium silicate (ZrSiO4), formed through reaction between zirconia and silica oxides. Amorphous SiO2 glass phase was also present but not detected by X-ray diffraction.
evidence_any: The surface of the samples after ablation is composed of monoclinic ZrO2

questions.question: According to Zapata-Solvas et al. 2013, what specific temperature-dependent thermal transport mechanism did lanthanum oxide addition provide to stabilize the heat flow properties of zirconium diboride-silicon carbide composite materials at ultra-high temperatures?
questions.reference_answer: Zapata-Solvas et al. 2013 attributed the constant thermal conductivity of La2O3-SiC-doped ZrB2 from 1500°C to 1900°C to La2O3's high coefficient of thermal expansion (10.2 × 10^-6 K^-1), which prevented contact loss between SiC particles and matrix grains during heating. This maintained consistent Kapitza resistance at grain boundaries, unlike SiC-doped ZrB2 alone where thermal conductivity decreased by 7% over the same temperature range.
evidence_any: La2O3 prevents contact loss between SiC grains and the matrix grains

questions.question: In SHAO et al. 2023, how did increasing the zirconium content in the multi-component protective coating affect the dimensions and spatial arrangement of the individual crystalline particles within the deposited layer before heating exposure?
questions.reference_answer: SHAO et al. 2023 found that higher zirconium content (60 wt% Zr in ZSM-H) produced significantly smaller particle sizes of zirconium carbide and silicon carbide within the coating compared to lower zirconium content (34 wt% Zr in ZSM-L), and the particles were distributed more homogeneously throughout the matrix.
evidence_any: the particle sizes of ZrC and SiC within the ZSM-H coating are smaller

questions.question: In Zapata-Solvas et al. 2013, what trend did the rate of heat diffusion through ultra-high-temperature zirconium diboride and hafnium diboride materials display as temperature increased from room conditions to 1900°C, and how did rare earth and silicon carbide doping alter this temperature dependence?
questions.reference_answer: In Zapata-Solvas et al. 2013, thermal diffusivity of both ZrB2-SiC and HfB2-SiC composites decreased with increasing temperature from room temperature to 1900°C. Importantly, La2O3-SiC-doped UHTCs showed only a 4% reduction in thermal diffusivity compared to 10% reduction in SiC-doped UHTCs alone, demonstrating that La2O3 addition stabilized the heat diffusion coefficient at elevated temperatures.
evidence_any: the reduction of thermal diffusivity from 1500 ◦C to 1900 ◦C is ∼10% for SiC-doped UHTCs

questions.question: In SHAO et al. 2023 research on protective ceramic coatings for composite materials, how did the dominant degradation mechanisms of the zirconium carbide-silicon carbide-molybdenum disilicide coating transform across three sequential stages as the intensity of the oxyacetylene flame environment progressively increased?
questions.reference_answer: SHAO et al. 2023 identified three sequential ablation mechanisms for the ZSM coatings. In the initial stage, oxidation of carbides was the primary mechanism, consuming oxygen and controlling the ablation rate. In the middle stage, oxidation combined with evaporation of silica phases dominated, with diffusion of oxygen through the oxide layer controlling the rate. In the final stage, ablation was controlled by a combination of oxidation, evaporation, and mechanical denudation from high-speed gas flow.
evidence_any: The ablation behavior of the coatings varies with different ablation conditions

questions.question: In Zapata-Solvas et al. 2013, how did the spatial arrangement and connectivity of silicon carbide particles within the diboride matrix material correlate with the heat transport efficiency measured at elevated temperatures for both zirconium-diboride and hafnium-diboride based composites?
questions.reference_answer: Zapata-Solvas et al. 2013 found that homogeneously dispersed and well-connected SiC networks in ZrB2-based composites produced higher thermal conductivity compared to monolithic ZrB2, whereas SiC agglomerates in HfB2-based composites created Kapitza resistance barriers that reduced thermal conductivity below that of monolithic HfB2, demonstrating that secondary phase dispersion plays a leading role in thermal transport efficiency.
evidence_any: dispersion of secondary phase throughout the composite volume plays a leading role in thermal

questions.question: In Zapata-Solvas et al. 2013, what was the role of the coefficient of thermal expansion differential between lanthanum oxide and silicon carbide phases in explaining the measured behavior of heat transport properties in the rare-earth-doped ultra-high-temperature ceramic composites?
questions.reference_answer: Zapata-Solvas et al. 2013 attributed constant thermal conductivity in La2O3-SiC-doped ZrB2 to controlled thermal expansion mismatch. La2O3 has the highest coefficient of thermal expansion (10.2 × 10^-6 K^-1) while SiC has the smallest (3.3 × 10^-6 K^-1). During heating, this mismatch allowed La2O3 to prevent contact loss between SiC and matrix grains, maintaining consistent Kapitza resistance at grain interfaces across the 1500-1900°C temperature range.
evidence_any: La2O3 CTE is the highest (10.2 × 10−6 K−1), while SiC CTE is

questions.question: In SHAO et al. 2023, what roles did the aluminum oxide and cerium dioxide sintering aid additives play in achieving dense, homogeneously distributed multi-phase protective coatings on carbon composite substrates via slurry painting and high-temperature sintering?
questions.reference_answer: In SHAO et al. 2023, aluminum oxide promoted solid-state reactions and prevented coating cracking by combining with SiC to inhibit microcrack expansion, while cerium dioxide acted as a surfactant with high chemical activity. Together, Al2O3 and CeO2 improved wettability toward SiC and ZrC phases, increasing the driving force for sintering and promoting coating densification and homogeneous distribution of ZrC and SiC particles throughout the matrix.
evidence_any: Al2O3 can promote solid reaction and prevent coating from cracking by

questions.question: In the Hu et al. 2023 air-plasma-sprayed ZrB2-based ultra-high-temperature ceramic coatings, what chemical element becomes depleted during the high-temperature treatment process, and what oxide compound derived from this element remains in the sprayed deposit?
questions.reference_answer: Boron (B) element is lost from ZrB2 during air plasma spraying, while boron trioxide (B2O3) residue accumulates in the coating. The B element loss increases surface temperature and reduces the sintering of the zirconia outer layer, while the residual B2O3 evaporates at high temperature causing porosity damage.
evidence_any: B element loss and residual B2O3 in the sprayed ZrB2 coating were observed

questions.question: In the Hu et al. 2023 study of air-plasma-sprayed ZrB2 coatings, what quantitative phase composition was measured in the treated powder using reference intensity ratio analysis, and what three distinct crystalline or amorphous phases comprise the sprayed deposit?
questions.reference_answer: The sprayed ZrB2 powder contains approximately 42 vol% unreacted ZrB2, 53 vol% monoclinic zirconia (m-ZrO2), and 5 vol% amorphous boron trioxide (B2O3). These proportions were determined through XRD analysis and confirmed by thermogravimetric studies showing reduced weight gain rates compared to pristine powder.
evidence_any: the phase composition of sprayed ZrB2 powder can be inferred as 42 vol.%ZrB2, 53 vol.% m-ZrO2, and 5 vol.% B2O3

questions.question: In Hu et al. 2023's investigation of ablation-resistant coatings incorporating titanium carbide (TaC) as a reinforcing phase alongside zirconium diboride, how did this secondary phase affect the detrimental effects of boron-oxide compound formation during thermal testing?
questions.reference_answer: In the ZrB2-TaC coating system studied by Hu et al. 2023, the tantalum carbide secondary phase reduced the negative effects of residual boron trioxide. The TaC oxidizes to form a low-eutectic Zr-Ta-O glass layer with improved heat-dissipation capacity compared to pure zirconia, which weakens the structural damage from boron trioxide evaporation.
evidence_any: the improved heat dissipation capacity of the Zr-Ta-O layer than the ZrO2 layer will weaken its negative effects

questions.question: In Hu et al. 2023, what measurement technique was used to quantify the resistance of ZrB2-based ceramic coatings to high-temperature erosion and mass loss, and what were the specific testing parameters employed in this evaluation method?
questions.reference_answer: Hu et al. 2023 employed oxyacetylene flame ablation testing to assess erosion resistance, using a constant thermal flux of 2.4 megawatts per square meter applied for 60 seconds. A two-color infrared thermometer measured surface temperatures during the test, with measurements taken at specific time points to track the material's performance under extreme thermal conditions.
evidence_any: The heat flux was 2.4 MW/m2 and the ablation time for all the samples lasted 60 s.

questions.question: In Zhang et al. 2019's directionally solidified ZrB2-SiC ultra-high-temperature ceramic composites, what was the characteristic architecture of the reinforcement phase at the microscale, and what was its measured thickness dimension?
questions.reference_answer: The Zhang et al. 2019 directionally solidified ZrB2-SiC eutectic composite exhibits a distinctive lamellar arrangement of alternating silicon carbide and zirconium diboride layers. The individual lamellae have a thickness of approximately 5 micrometers, creating a highly aligned microstructure.
evidence_any: The bright phase is SiC and the dark phase is ZrB2. The lamella thickness is about 5 μm.

questions.question: In Zhang et al. 2019's investigation of high-temperature air-exposure resistance in directionally solidified ZrB2-SiC composites, what was the magnitude of the protective oxide layer formed on different sample surfaces at 1873 Kelvin exposure, and how did orientation affect this protective layer thickness?
questions.reference_answer: At 1873 K (1600 degrees Celsius), the directionally solidified ZrB2-SiC composite formed oxide scales of dramatically different thicknesses depending on surface orientation. The side surface (parallel to the lamellar growth direction) developed a thin protective layer of 65 micrometers, while the top surface perpendicular to growth showed a thicker, less protective oxide scale of 205 micrometers due to differences in silicon carbide orientation.
evidence_any: The measured oxide scales thicknesses are given in Table 1

questions.question: In Zhang et al. 2019's directionally solidified ZrB2-SiC composite, what quantitative measure of crystallographic alignment was achieved, and how does this numerical descriptor characterize the degree of grain orientation in the material?
questions.reference_answer: Zhang et al. 2019 achieved a Lotgering orientation factor as high as 0.97 in their directionally solidified ZrB2-SiC composite, measured from the (00l) diffraction peaks. The Lotgering factor on a scale from zero to one quantifies the preferential crystallographic orientation of grains, where 1.0 represents perfect alignment and 0.97 indicates excellent texturing of the grain structure.
evidence_any: The (00l) Lotgering orientation factor was as high as 0.97

questions.question: In Hu et al. 2023's ablation studies of ZrB2-SiC coatings at high thermal flux, what phenomenon was observed when comparing the outer-layer temperature of boron-rich ceramic samples to boron-depleted coating samples during thermal exposure?
questions.reference_answer: Hu et al. 2023 observed that sprayed ZrB2-SiC coatings with depleted boron content paradoxically exhibited lower surface temperatures during ablation testing compared to dense sintered ZrB2-SiC ceramics with abundant boron. This unexpected temperature reduction is attributed to evaporation of the residual boron trioxide phase in the spray-processed coating, which extracts substantial thermal energy from the surface.
evidence_any: the unusual decline might be attributed to the evaporation of the residual B2O3 phase in the sprayed ZrB2 coatings

questions.question: In the 2022 study by Dubey et al. on HfB2-ZrB2-SiC-CNT composites, what mechanism dominates the reduction of heat transport capacity when hafnium and zirconium diboride phases intermix to form a hybrid crystalline structure?
questions.reference_answer: Phononic scattering due to the formation of (Hf-Zr)B2 solid solution dominates the reduction of thermal conductivity in HfB2-ZrB2 composites, accounting for approximately 44% of the total effect. The intermixing of hafnium and zirconium atoms creates lattice distortions that preferentially scatter acoustic vibrations traveling through the crystal lattice.
evidence_any: the domination of phononic scattering (by ~44%) due to solid solutioning in HfB2-ZrB2-based ceramics

questions.question: According to Dubey 2022, how much does the rate of heat diffusion through HfB2-containing composites (H20S sample) change compared to ZrB2-based materials (Z20S sample) despite the HfB2 system having superior density?
questions.reference_answer: The rate of heat diffusion (thermal conductivity) in HfB2-SiC composites is 18% lower than in ZrB2-SiC despite HfB2 having 100% density, compared to ZrB2 which shows some porosity. This reduction is attributed to poor sintering conditions in the H20S system that were not optimized for hafnium diboride, and to differences in how acoustic vibrations scatter at the interfaces and grain boundaries in hafnium diboride.
evidence_any: κH20S is 18% less than κZ20S despite being 100% dense, which may be attributed to poor sintering of H20S

questions.question: In Dubey 2022, what is the magnitude of enhancement to heat transport when 6 volume percent of carbon nanotube reinforcement is added to HfB2-ZrB2-SiC composites?
questions.reference_answer: The addition of 6 vol% carbon nanotubes to HfB2-ZrB2-SiC composites increases thermal conductivity by approximately 9%. This modest enhancement arises from the combined effects of porosity reduction (from 4% to 0.5%) and the intrinsically high thermal conductivity of carbon nanotubes (1000-2000 Wm-1K-1), though the effect is limited by random nanotube distribution.
evidence_any: Incorporation of highly conducting CNTs (~6 vol%) raised the κHZ20S by ~9% in HZ20S6C

questions.question: According to Balat-Pichelin 2014, what role does the surface tendency to promote rapid combination of oxygen atoms play in the thermal environment of hypersonic vehicles?
questions.reference_answer: Low catalytic activity for oxygen atom recombination reduces exothermic heating at the material surface during re-entry. When surface catalycity is high, oxygen atoms recombine rapidly releasing heat energy, which locally increases surface temperature. Conversely, low catalycity prevents this exothermic recombination process, thereby maintaining lower surface temperatures and reducing thermal stress on the protection system.
evidence_any: High emissivity and low surface catalycity towards the exothermal recombination of oxygen atoms

questions.question: What two distinct sintering additives are compared in the Balat-Pichelin 2014 study of ZrB2-fiber reinforced silicon carbide ceramics, and what microstructural role do they play?
questions.reference_answer: The two sintering additives are silicon nitride (Si3N4) and zirconium silicide (ZrSi2). These additives modify the microstructural development during the high-temperature consolidation process, affecting grain growth, phase distribution, and interfacial bonding between the ceramic matrix and fiber reinforcement, which in turn influences the surface properties and oxidation resistance.
evidence_any: ZrB2–SiCfiber composition with different sintering additives (Si3N4 or ZrSi2)

questions.question: What is the density of zirconium carbide (ZrC) in g/cm³ according to Kim et al. 2024 study on vacuum plasma spray coating of ZrC on carbon-carbon composites?
questions.reference_answer: Zirconium carbide has a density of 6.73 g/cm³.
evidence_any: ZrC exhibits excellent resistance to corrosion and possesses a density of 6.73 g/cm3

questions.question: In Table 1 of the Kim et al. 2024 study on powder size-dependent ZrC coating, what is the applied power in kilowatts for Case B with 49 μm powder at 50 mbar?
questions.reference_answer: Case B in Kim et al. 2024 applied a power of 54 kilowatts.

questions.question: What is the melting point temperature of zirconium carbide (ZrC) in degrees Celsius as stated in the Kim et al. 2024 paper on UHTC coatings?
questions.reference_answer: Zirconium carbide has a melting point of 3530 degrees Celsius.
evidence_any: zirconium carbide (ZrC) has a remarkably high melting point of 3530

questions.question: In Table 1 of Kim et al. 2024, what is the applied current in amperes for Case A with the largest powder size (49 μm) at 50 mbar chamber pressure?
questions.reference_answer: Case A in the Kim et al. 2024 study used a current of 630 amperes.

questions.question: What is the chamber pressure in millibar for Case D in Table 1 of the Kim et al. 2024 vacuum plasma spray study on ZrC powder coatings?
questions.reference_answer: Case D in Kim et al. 2024 operated at a chamber pressure of 100 millibar.

questions.question: According to Figure 6 in Kim et al. 2024, what is the ZrC coating layer thickness in micrometers achieved in Case F using 33 μm powder at 150 mbar chamber pressure?
questions.reference_answer: Case F in the Kim et al. 2024 study achieved a ZrC coating thickness of 50 micrometers.
evidence_any: Case F exhibited the highest thickness (50 μm) and very smooth roughness

questions.question: According to Table 2 in Thimmappa et al. 2021, what is the specific weight gain in mg/cm² for the ZrB2-20 vol% SiC-2.5 wt% Ta (ZS-2.5Ta) composition after high temperature oxidation testing?
questions.reference_answer: The ZrB2-20 vol% SiC-2.5 wt% Ta composition shows a specific weight gain of 21.04 mg/cm² after oxidation at 1600 degrees Celsius for 10 hours according to Thimmappa et al. 2021.

questions.question: In Table 1 of Thimmappa et al. 2021, what is the relative density percentage for the ZrB2-20 vol% SiC-10 wt% Ta (ZS-10Ta) composition processed at 1900°C?
questions.reference_answer: The ZrB2-20 vol% SiC-10 wt% Ta composition achieved a relative density of 99.63 percent according to Thimmappa et al. 2021.
evidence_any: ZrB2-20 vol% SiC-10 wt% Ta (ZS-10Ta) 99.63

questions.question: What was the density of the Xu et al. 2021 ZF-ZS composite sample prepared by reactive infiltration of K2ZrF6 molten salt?
questions.reference_answer: The ZF-ZS composite sample had a density of 3.55 ± 0.05 g/cm³.

questions.question: In Xu et al. 2021, what was the mass ablation rate of the ZF-ZS C/C-UHTC composite when tested under oxyacetylene flame at 2800 degrees Celsius surface temperature for 60 seconds?
questions.reference_answer: The ZF-ZS composite prepared by Xu and colleagues exhibited a mass ablation rate of 2.6 ± 0.02 mg/s.
evidence_any: ZF-ZS Molten salt + RMI O2:1.96 L/s, C2H2:0.696 L/s, Surface 2800 ℃ 60 2.6±0.02 0.82 ±0.04

questions.question: According to Xu et al. 2021 Table 2, what was the linear ablation rate in micrometers per second for the ZF-ZS composite material at a flame temperature of 2800 degrees Celsius?
questions.reference_answer: The ZF-ZS composite had a linear ablation rate of 0.82 ± 0.04 μm/s under oxyacetylene flame ablation.
evidence_any: ZF-ZS Molten salt + RMI O2:1.96 L/s, C2H2:0.696 L/s, Surface 2800 ℃ 60 2.6±0.02 0.82 ±0.04

questions.question: What was the mass ablation rate reported by Xu et al. 2021 for the TF-S C/C-Ti3SiC2-SiC composite material tested at 2800 degrees Celsius surface temperature?
questions.reference_answer: The TF-S composite sample had a mass ablation rate of 17.3 ± 0.58 mg/s.

questions.question: In the Xu et al. 2021 study, what was the open porosity percentage of the ZF-S composite prepared by reactive infiltration of K2ZrF6 molten salt?
questions.reference_answer: The ZF-S sample had an open porosity of 7.6%.

questions.question: According to Xu et al. 2021 Table 2, what was the mass ablation rate of the C/C-ZrC-SiC composite prepared by ICVI plus reactive melt infiltration?
questions.reference_answer: The C/C-ZrC-SiC composite prepared by ICVI and RMI had a mass ablation rate of 0.21 mg/s.
evidence_any: C/C-ZrC-SiC ICVI + RMI O2:0.21 L/s, C2H2:0.155 L/s, Flame 2300 ℃ 90 0.21 1.44

questions.question: What was the Vickers hardness of the reference TiB2-SiC ceramic sample without any carbon additive (TS00) in the Popov et al. 2024 study?
questions.reference_answer: The reference TS00 TiB2-SiC ceramic without carbon additives had a Vickers hardness of 22.5 ± 1 GPa.
